other_observers: Skyler and Cree Bol; To my knowledge, I am the original finder. Rachel Hopper saw this bird and provide the attach photos. I believe Nick Komar and Cole Wild, perhaps others, saw this bird in the next few days.
specific_location: Crow Valley Campground, about 75 yards northeast of the gate that marks the west entrance to the primitive camping area traversed by the east-west "Mourning Dove Trail". There is a temporary wetland depression lined with mesh wire just south of the willow thicket in which the sparrow flock sought for cover. When they came out, they would move to the west into the dried, weedy margins of an area wet earlier in the year toward some big timbers marking the edge of the drainage near a few decrepid old cottonwoods, sunflowers, and a fence.
description: Large sparrow, long-tailed, similar in size and overall shape to nearby White-crowned Sparrows. Head rather flat-crowned in side profile with a slight "ruffle" at the rear crown (sort of like Steve Lavin, coach of St. John's (see ESPN) on a bad day when his jell is not holding in the back). Side of face rather plain, buffy tan. Complete, not overly conspicuous white eyering. Eye black. Rear of auricular area somewhat darker gray-brown than main part, similar to that of winter Lapland Longspur. Bill thick and bi-colored: upper mandible grayish yellow, lower bright yellow. In front-on view, crown dark brown and gray striped (2 stripes?) on the sides, and yellow and brown striped in the center. Yellow not all that evident except for perfect straight ahead, close views. Bird had a light gray "collared" look from below the auriculars on the side of the lower head around to the throat. Lower throat, breast and belly light gray brown and unstriped. Back (mantle) black and buffy-tan striped. Rump grayish tan. Scapulars not seen all that well. Greater coverts with broad, pale (tan fading to white) margins, entirely black centers. Secondaries and primaries similar to greater coverts, with tips of primaries being mostly black with white margins. Under tail coverts appeared mostly white (and unstriped?). Tail mostly blackish on top, long. (see photos)Behaviors: Very unwilling to give good views. Stayed in sunflowers and coyote willows, once sat on fence wire briefly, skulky, influenced by hyper White-crown flock which, likewise, seemed paranoid. Rachel Hopper's photos submitted with this report show it atop a fence post, somewhat obscured by willow foliage. This area was north of the campground on the edge of a temporary wetland and the open prairie.Call: not heard that I know of (bird was mixed in with a large flock of White-crowned Sparrows and I did not note it making vocalizations distinguishable from the others)Plumage: first-winter
photographer: Photographer: Rachel Hopper, Rachel Hopper, Rachel Hopper
